name: Becharof Lake
short_description: Remote volcanic area near Alaska's largest freshwater lake offering obsidian and chalcedony finds. Access by floatplane or boat only - a true backcountry rockhounding adventure.
description:
Becharof Lake sits in the volcanic heart of the Lake and Peninsula Borough, Alaska's most remote rockhounding destinations. The lake basin and surrounding terrain were shaped by Tertiary-era volcanic activity and subsequent glaciation, creating ideal conditions for obsidian and chalcedony formation. The flat tundra surrounding the lake exposes volcanic materials along shorelines and in scattered outcrops, making this an exceptional site for advanced rockhounds willing to undertake significant travel logistics.

This location is accessible only by floatplane or boat from King Salmon or other coastal Alaska towns, making it a true expedition destination. The surrounding area is pristine wilderness with minimal human activity, offering solitude and opportunity to explore relatively untouched volcanic terrain. Water-worn specimens are common along the lake shore where wave action has exposed and polished obsidian fragments over millennia.

The geological setting reflects Becharof Lake's position within Alaska's volcanic arc system. Miocene-age volcanism deposited rhyolitic lavas and pyroclastic materials that fractured into the sharp, glassy obsidian prized by collectors. Glacial processes then distributed these materials across the landscape and into modern shoreline deposits.

Expect challenging conditions: remote location, unpredictable Alaska weather, and wildlife presence. However, the combination of volcanic geology, minimal competition from other rockhounders, and the rugged beauty of the Aleutian Range make this bucket-list material for serious collectors.
history: Becharof Lake and the surrounding Lake and Peninsula Borough sit atop the Aleutian volcanic arc, created by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the North American Plate. Tertiary-age rhyolitic volcanism (approximately 50-20 million years ago) deposited the silica-rich lava flows and ash deposits from which obsidian and chalcedony crystallized. Glacial advancement and retreat during the Pleistocene further exposed and distributed these volcanic materials across the lake basin and surrounding terrain.
tips: Search along the shoreline of Becharof Lake where glacial and wave action has concentrated obsidian fragments and chalcedony in gravel bars and water-worn deposits. Look for the glassy, black obsidian that reflects light distinctively - it often occurs in fist-sized to palm-sized pieces. Bring a magnet and UV light: obsidian is non-magnetic, helping distinguish it from magnetite, and some specimens may fluoresce weakly under UV. Pack extra water and food, as resupply options do not exist at the lake - plan for complete self-sufficiency during your stay.
terrain_notes: Becharof Lake sits at 33 feet elevation with predominantly flat tundra terrain surrounding it. The landscape is open, treeless, and often boggy with low-growing vegetation, sedges, and mosses. Walking is generally easy across the tundra but can be wet and spongy. Shoreline access involves gentle slopes down to the water's edge where obsidian and chalcedony fragments concentrate. No established trails exist. Weather exposure is complete - prepare for wind, rain, and rapid temperature changes even in summer. The lake itself is subject to sudden storms and rough water conditions.
best_season: June - August. This is the only period when floatplanes operate reliably to Becharof Lake and when weather is minimally unpredictable. Winter conditions are extreme with heavy snow and ice. Late spring and early fall present increased storm risk and shorter operational windows for air transport. Summer provides 16+ hours of daylight, essential for maximizing productive rockhounding time.
written_directions: Becharof Lake is accessed via floatplane charter from King Salmon (approximately 130 miles southwest) or Dillingham (approximately 150 miles northwest). There are no roads to Becharof Lake. From King Salmon, contact local air charter services such as Branch River Air or similar operators to arrange floatplane transport to the lake. Journey time is approximately 45 minutes to 1.5 hours depending on exact landing location. Upon arrival, rockhounding is conducted on foot from the floatplane landing site along the shorelines and across the tundra surrounding the lake basin. Arrange pickup with your charter service in advance.
parking_notes: No parking area exists. Floatplane lands directly on Becharof Lake. Establish a base camp near the landing site and conduct day hikes along the shoreline and across surrounding tundra.
collection_rules: Rockhounding on public lands surrounding Becharof Lake falls under general Alaska public land collection guidelines. Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per person per day. No commercial collection. No mechanized equipment. Respect all wildlife protection areas and any seasonal closures related to fish and game management. Consult with Alaska Department of Fish and Game and the Bureau of Land Management before your trip for any current restrictions.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Maximum 25 lbs per person per day
No commercial collection permitted
No mechanized excavation or power tools
Respect wildlife and seasonal closures
Verify current restrictions with Alaska DEC and BLM before traveling
nearest_services: King Salmon (floatplane hub) - approximately 130 miles northeast by air. Full services including fuel, food, lodging, and air charter operations. Alternatively Dillingham, approximately 150 miles northwest by air.
accessibility_notes: This is an expert-level backcountry rockhounding destination requiring significant physical capability and wilderness experience. Terrain is open tundra with no trails, boggy and wet conditions, and exposure to harsh weather. Floatplane access requires careful logistics and willingness to navigate remote Alaska conditions. Not suitable for families with young children, individuals with limited mobility, or those without strong wilderness skills. Expect full exposure to wind, rain, cold, and wildlife. Proper gear including heavy rain/insulation, navigation equipment, and emergency supplies are essential.

faq.question: What types of obsidian and chalcedony can I find at Becharof Lake, Alaska?
faq.answer: Becharof Lake is known for high-quality black obsidian and red to brown chalcedony specimens. The obsidian occurs in glassy, sharp-edged fragments ranging from fist-sized to palm-sized pieces, often water-worn and rounded by glacial and wave action along the shoreline. Chalcedony appears in banded specimens with warm earth tones. These materials result from Tertiary-age rhyolitic volcanism in Alaska's volcanic arc.

faq.question: How do I get to Becharof Lake for rockhounding - is there a road?
faq.answer: There are no roads to Becharof Lake. Access is exclusively by floatplane charter from King Salmon (130 miles northeast) or Dillingham (150 miles northwest). Journey time is 45 minutes to 1.5 hours. You must arrange a floatplane charter before arrival and schedule your pickup date in advance. This remote access makes Becharof Lake a true expedition destination for serious rockhounds.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Becharof Lake?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal-use rockhounding at Becharof Lake on public lands. However, you must follow Alaska's general collection guidelines: 25 lbs per person per day maximum, personal use only, and no commercial collection. Consult with the Alaska Department of Fish and Game and BLM before traveling to verify any seasonal closures or wildlife restrictions.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Becharof Lake?
faq.answer: June through August is the only practical season for rockhounding at Becharof Lake. This window provides the most reliable weather, longest daylight hours (16+ hours), and consistent floatplane service. Winter is extreme with heavy snow and ice. Spring and fall face increased storm risk and limited air charter availability. Summer maximizes your productive rockhounding time in this remote Alaska location.

faq.question: What tools and gear should I bring for rockhounding Becharof Lake?
faq.answer: Bring a geology hammer, hand lens, UV light, magnet, and collection bags. Critical: pack heavy rain gear, insulated layers, wind-resistant jacket, sturdy hiking boots, and emergency supplies. Cell service does not exist here - bring a satellite communicator. Food and water must be packed for your entire stay. Navigation GPS or maps are essential. There is no resupply available at the lake - plan for complete self-sufficiency.

faq.question: Is Becharof Lake rockhounding suitable for beginners?
faq.answer: No. Becharof Lake is an expert-level destination requiring significant wilderness experience, backcountry navigation skills, and physical stamina. The remote location, harsh weather exposure, complete isolation, and wildlife presence make this suitable only for experienced rockhounds and outdoor adventurers. Beginners should start with more accessible Alaska rockhounding sites closer to populated areas before attempting an expedition to Becharof Lake.

meta_description: Find black obsidian and chalcedony at remote Becharof Lake, Alaska. Floatplane access only. Expert wilderness rockhounding expedition destination.
